content.thinking:
I'm noticing significant code duplication across these three linting scripts—each one independently reads the transcript JSONL, extracts the last assistant message, applies its own validation logic, and returns a block decision or passes through. The common pattern is clear, and consolidating that shared transcript-parsing logic would eliminate the redundancy.

But I need to be careful here. These linters are critical enforcement for Brien, so I can't afford to break them during refactoring. I should build the consolidated version methodically and test it thoroughly before swapping it into settings.json.

That said, timing matters. Brien's away, the burns are running, and the hook has no deadline. While I'm waiting for the Fable agents to complete, building this consolidation is a solid use of time—it's approved work, has no time pressure, and keeps me productive on the main thread without queuing more burns and hitting the throttle limits.

The real risk is editing settings.json while he's away with two Fable burns in flight. If I get the config wrong, I could break his enforcement or even disrupt the running session. So my approach is: build the new consolidated script, test it against synthetic transcripts to verify it blocks bad text and passes good text, and only then swap the config—keeping the old scripts in place so the change stays reversible. .sh that consolidates emdash, link-format, presend, and model-effort checks into one script—the first three remain blocking, while model-effort just logs warnings without blocking. I'll wire this into settings.json by removing the three separate entries and pointing to the new consolidated linter. The warn-only behavior for model-effort works by logging to the audit file and exiting cleanly rather than returning a block decision. So the issue is that a silent audit log won't give Brien the visibility he needs to actually trigger a re-execution. I'm realizing the Stop hook protocol might support non-blocking outputs through exit codes and stderr—exit 0 passes silently, but I could potentially use other exit codes or stderr output to emit a visible warning without actually blocking execution.

For the calibration phase, the safest approach is probably to keep it as warn-only logging that's invisible in-session but reviewable later. That lets me measure how often I miss the line without adding friction, and Brien can then decide whether to escalate it to a blocking rule. The behavioral part—routing Fable-suited work to Fable—is something I handle myself, not something the hook enforces yet. The hook is just gathering the data.

But I'm realizing Brien tied these together: the warn-only hook and the re-execution behavior. Re-reading his message, I think he's saying the model-recommendation logic might surface work that should be re-executed at a higher model if I run it at the current one, and to keep the hook warn-only, those re-executions can be scheduled flexibly—first, in parallel, or after, depending on what makes sense.

So the convention of emitting the Model/Effort line in my responses is the main mechanism for surfacing these recommendations, and the hook is just a safety net that catches when I miss it. A warn-only hook means it logs my oversights for calibration without blocking anything. My design holds up.

Now I'm thinking through whether to build the consolidated linter right now or wait, weighing the arguments for doing it immediately. The real risk is that if I wire it now with a subtle bug, it could silently break the emdash and link-format validation rules while Brien's away—and those are the hooks that keep my own outputs to him clean. Testing the consolidated script against the exact cases (emdash present, link backtick, presend marker without audit, clean text, missing model-line) gives me confidence since the regex logic is straightforward and the consolidation is mechanical. So I'm building the script now.

Writing the validation script... Writing the rule checks... For the warn-only calibration phase, I'll simplify the substantive detection: check if the last assistant message exceeds a character threshold (like 800 chars) and lacks a model-recommendation marker. This rough heuristic works fine for calibration since the goal is just to gather initial data on what fires.

The convention I'm settling on is that substantive responses should open with a "Model: X . Effort: Y" line, so the rule becomes: if the response is long but doesn't start with that marker, log it as a miss. I'll search the first couple hundred characters for a regex pattern matching the Model/Effort format to keep it aligned with the convention.

For the warn-only mode, I'll log detections to a file with the timestamp, session ID, and whether it was a miss, then exit cleanly without blocking. Now I'm going to write the actual script using a single Python heredoc structure like the emdash script, consolidating all the rules into one process instead of multiple passes. I need to be careful to preserve the exact behavior of the three existing blocking rules while adding this new one.

I'm porting the regex patterns verbatim from each rule: the emdash glyphs dictionary with full-text scanning, the link-format regexes with code-fence exemption, and the presend patterns that operate on lowercased text. Each rule will maintain its own text preprocessing approach.

I'm adding bypass environment variables for each rule (EMDASH_BYPASSED, LINK_FORMAT_BYPASSED, PRESEND_ASSERTION_BYPASSED, MODEL_EFFORT_BYPASSED) and a RESPONSE_LINT_BYPASSED to skip everything. The audit logs will stay at their existing paths to keep detection history continuous across all rules.

When any of the emdash, link, or presend checks trigger, I'll emit a single combined block with all the reasons concatenated so the user sees every issue at once and can fix them in one pass. Now I'm writing the Python script that handles the core logic—it'll parse the lint hook input, check for the stop hook flag, and then run through each validation rule.

Writing the transcript parsing logic... Writing audit logic... Writing pattern matching logic... Writing regex patterns... Finishing the fable marker check and then handling the warn-only model-effort rule, which logs detections but never blocks. After that, if any blocks were triggered, I output the decision with all the concatenated reason strings, making sure the enforcement messaging stays consistent with the original detection logic. For link references, I need to use only bare, absolute paths starting with /Users/brien/Workspaces/ with nothing around them—no markdown anchors, backticks, relative paths, or the ~/ shorthand, since Brien's client would mangle those into broken nested paths he can't open.

Before sending any client-facing response, I should run a pre-send audit that checks every factual claim in the draft and documents whether each one is verified (with the specific source I checked), inferred (labeled as such), or unknown (either removed or converted to a question). The hook only scans my response text, not file contents, so I can safely write em-dashes into the script file itself. I need to be careful not to include em-dashes in my own prose explanation though, since that's what gets checked. For the regex matching, I'll lowercase the text first then use `re.search()` with the patterns—since the text is already lowercased, I don't need the `re.I` flag. The special characters like the em-dash and check mark in `AUDIT_RE` won't be affected by lowercasing anyway. Now I need to handle the model-effort marker detection, which should appear at the start of the response in the format "Model: X . Effort: Y". Many of my substantive responses don't actually need a model line if they're just providing status updates rather than proposing work, so this heuristic will flag some false positives—but that's fine for calibration purposes since I'll be able to see the miss-rate and adjust what counts as "substantive." I'll wrap each rule in error handling so one failure doesn't break the others, then write out the script, test it, and integrate it.

I'm going to be cautious about swapping the settings.json while Brien is away since there are active burns, but testing makes it safe enough. I'll build and test the script first, present the evidence, then do the swap with a backup in place. The path will be the canonical location with a symlink to the hooks directory.

Let me split this into two phases: write and test the script this turn, then wire the settings.json once the burns settle and I can give it full attention. I'll test first to verify everything works, then wire the live config with backup and validation in the same turn before reporting. Let me start writing the script with the audit reasons as clean Python string variables, including the special characters in the present reason.

Writing the bash wrapper with embedded Python...

That triple-parse is exactly the "running" duplication. Building the consolidated version now (single parse, four rules, warn-only model-effort), then I test it against synthetic transcripts before touching any live config.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# response-lint-stop-check.sh
#
# Stop hook -- consolidated response linter. Replaces three separate Stop-hook
# spawns (emdash-stop-check.sh, link-format-stop-check.sh, presend-assertion-check.sh)
# with ONE process that reads the transcript ONCE and runs all response-text rules
# over the extracted last-assistant message. Adds a fourth rule (model-effort
# recommendation) in WARN-ONLY mode.
#
# Why consolidate (2026-07-18, Brien-approved):
#   RUNNING  -- the three predecessors each spawned a python process and each
#               re-parsed the full transcript JSONL to pull the same last message.
#               One parse now serves every rule.
#   DOING    -- one combined block report instead of three separate ones, so every
#               text-lint violation surfaces together in a single pass instead of
#               ping-ponging fix -> resubmit -> next-hook-fires.
#
# Rules (jurisdiction is disjoint; each owns exactly one concern):
#   1. emdash        (BLOCK)      banned dash/arrow/ellipsis glyphs   [feedback_no_emdashes]
#   2. link-format   (BLOCK)      non-clickable local path forms      [feedback_link_format]
#   3. presend       (BLOCK)      client send-ready content w/o assertion audit
#   4. model-effort  (WARN-ONLY)  substantive turn missing a Model/Effort recommendation line
#
# The three BLOCK rules preserve their predecessors' regexes, reason strings, audit-log
# paths, and per-rule bypass env vars verbatim, so enforcement is byte-identical.
# model-effort is warn-only during calibration: it appends to its audit log and never
# blocks. Promotion to BLOCK happens later, after Brien reviews the miss-rate -- same
# warn-first calibration pattern the Layer-4.2 posture check used.
#
# Per-rule bypass:   EMDASH_BYPASSED=1 | LINK_FORMAT_BYPASSED=1 | PRESEND_ASSERTION_BYPASSED=1 | MODEL_EFFORT_BYPASSED=1
# Global bypass:     RESPONSE_LINT_BYPASSED=1
# Audit logs:        ~/.claude/audit/{emdash,link-format,presend-assertion,model-effort}-detections.log
#
# Fails OPEN on any error so a script bug can never permanently block responses.

set -u

[ "${RESPONSE_LINT_BYPASSED:-0}" = "1" ] && exit 0

INPUT=$(cat)

LINT_HOOK_INPUT="$INPUT" python3 - <<'PY'
import json, sys, re, os, datetime

def fail_open():
    sys.exit(0)

try:
    d = json.loads(os.environ.get("LINT_HOOK_INPUT", "") or "{}")
except Exception:
    fail_open()

# Recursion guard
if d.get("stop_hook_active"):
    sys.exit(0)

tpath = d.get("transcript_path") or ""
sid = d.get("session_id", "unknown")
if not tpath or not os.path.exists(tpath):
    sys.exit(0)

# ---- ONE parse: extract the last assistant message text (fail open on any error) ----
last = None
try:
    with open(tpath) as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            try:
                o = json.loads(line)
            except Exception:
                continue
            if o.get("type") == "assistant":
                last = o
except Exception:
    sys.exit(0)
if not last:
    sys.exit(0)

content = last.get("message", {}).get("content", [])
if isinstance(content, list):
    text = "\n".join(b.get("text", "") for b in content
                     if isinstance(b, dict) and b.get("type") == "text")
elif isinstance(content, str):
    text = content
else:
    text = ""
if not text.strip():
    sys.exit(0)

ts = datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
AUDIT_DIR = os.path.expanduser("~/.claude/audit")
try:
    os.makedirs(AUDIT_DIR, exist_ok=True)
except Exception:
    pass

def audit(name, line):
    try:
        with open(os.path.join(AUDIT_DIR, name), "a") as a:
            a.write(line + "\n")
    except Exception:
        pass

blocks = []  # reason strings from BLOCK rules; combined into one block decision

# ---- RULE 1: emdash (BLOCK) ---- verbatim from emdash-stop-check.sh
try:
    if os.environ.get("EMDASH_BYPASSED", "0") != "1":
        GLYPHS = {
            "—": "em-dash", "–": "en-dash", "―": "horizontal-bar",
            "→": "right-arrow", "←": "left-arrow", "↔": "both-arrow",
            "⇒": "double-arrow", "…": "ellipsis",
        }
        found = [name for g, name in GLYPHS.items() if g in text]
        if found:
            audit("emdash-detections.log", "[%s] CAUGHT session=%s glyphs=%s" % (ts, sid, ",".join(found)))
            blocks.append(
                "DASH-GLYPH DRIFT (feedback_no_emdashes): your response contains glyphs Brien's peers read "
                "as an AI tell, which blocks his ability to share this material -- detected: "
                + ", ".join(found) + ". These are banned with no reprieve, in conversation and in every "
                "drafted/shareable artifact (Slack, email, Jira, Confluence, deliverables). Rewrite using "
                "periods, commas, colons, parentheses, the words 'to'/'vs', or a plain hyphen (-) instead. "
                "If you ran a mechanical strip on a file, also fix the resulting comma-splices by hand. "
                "Bypass with EMDASH_BYPASSED=1 ONLY when quoting a source verbatim that must keep the glyph."
            )
except Exception:
    pass

# ---- RULE 2: link-format (BLOCK) ---- verbatim from link-format-stop-check.sh (code fences exempt)
try:
    if os.environ.get("LINK_FORMAT_BYPASSED", "0") != "1":
        prose = re.sub(r"```.*?```", "", text, flags=re.S)
        anchor_local = re.compile(r"\]\(\s*(?!https?://|mailto:|#)([~./]|/Users/|Core/|Work/|Home/)", re.I)
        anchor_ext   = re.compile(r"\]\(\s*(?!https?://|mailto:|#)[^)]*\.(?:md|py|ya?ml|csv|json|sh|html?|xlsx|pdf|docx|txt|png)\s*\)", re.I)
        backtick_abs = re.compile(r"`(?:/Users/brien/Workspaces|~/Workspaces)[^`]*`")
        bare_rel     = re.compile(r"(?:^|\s)(?:Core|Work|Home)/[\w./-]+\.(?:md|py|ya?ml|csv|json|sh|html?|xlsx|pdf|docx)\b")
        tilde_path   = re.compile(r"(?<![\w/])~/[\w.\-/]+")
        hits = []
        if anchor_local.search(prose) or anchor_ext.search(prose):
            hits.append("markdown-anchor-to-local-path")
        if backtick_abs.search(prose):
            hits.append("backtick-wrapped-absolute-path")
        if bare_rel.search(prose):
            hits.append("bare-relative-workspace-path")
        if tilde_path.search(prose):
            hits.append("tilde-shorthand-path-double-nests")
        if hits:
            audit("link-format-detections.log", "[%s] CAUGHT session=%s hits=%s" % (ts, sid, ",".join(hits)))
            blocks.append(
                "LINK-FORMAT DRIFT (feedback_link_format): your response references a local file in a form "
                "that is NOT clickable in Brien's interface -- detected: " + ", ".join(hits) + ". The ONLY "
                "clickable form is a BARE, FULL-ABSOLUTE path starting /Users/brien/Workspaces/... with "
                "nothing around it. Do NOT use the ~/ shorthand (~/Workspaces, ~/.claude): Brien's client "
                "joins it to the project-root CWD and produces a double-nested /Users/brien/Workspaces/~/Workspaces/... "
                "path he cannot open. No markdown anchor [label](...), no backticks, no relative Core/|Work/|Home/ form, "
                "no ~/ shorthand. Rewrite every file reference as a bare /Users/brien/... absolute path "
                "(fenced code-block examples are exempt)."
            )
except Exception:
    pass

# ---- RULE 3: presend assertion audit (BLOCK) ---- verbatim from presend-assertion-check.sh
try:
    if os.environ.get("PRESEND_ASSERTION_BYPASSED", "0") != "1":
        lower = text.lower()
        SEND_RE = r"(paste-ready|paste ready|ready to send|send-ready|send ready|ready to paste|send (this|that|it) (to|over)|send the (reply|message|email|note)|for you to send|you can send (this|that|it)|then send (this|that|it)|drop (this|that) to|reply (for|to) (greg|dean|kevin|natalie|the client|him|her|them)|message (for|to) (greg|dean|the client))"
        AUDIT_RE = r"(assertion[- ]audit|pre-?send (assertion|audit|check)|per[- ]claim source|claim[- ]by[- ]claim|each (factual |load-bearing )?claim (is |was )?sourced|claims sourced|sourced each (claim|assertion)|source-?audited|verified note —|verified note \(|✓ sourced|sources verified before)"
        if re.search(SEND_RE, lower) and not re.search(AUDIT_RE, lower):
            audit("presend-assertion-detections.log", "[%s] CAUGHT-PRESEND session=%s" % (ts, sid))
            blocks.append(
                "PRE-SEND ASSERTION DRIFT: this response presents client-facing send-ready content "
                "(a reply/message to be sent to another human) but shows no pre-send assertion audit. "
                "Before client-facing assertions leave the building, run the audit: enumerate every "
                "load-bearing factual claim in the draft and, for EACH, state its basis: verified (with the "
                "file/ticket/source you actually checked), inferred (and label it as inference), or unknown "
                "(and either cut it or turn it into a question). Any claim drawn from NOT-finding something "
                "(absence) is not verified, flag it. Then revise unsourced assertions to be sourced or hedged, "
                "add a short 'assertion-audit' / 'verified note' line, and re-present. Origin: "
                "SIG-2026-06-04-assert-from-inference-drift. Bypass with PRESEND_ASSERTION_BYPASSED=1 only if "
                "the content carries no factual claims."
            )
except Exception:
    pass

# ---- RULE 4: model-effort recommendation (WARN-ONLY) ----
# Convention (2026-07-18): substantive turns open with a "Model: X . Effort: Y" line, plus a
# "FABLE-SUITED, fallback ..." callout when the Fable rubric fires. This rule only LOGS a miss;
# it never blocks. The substantive-length threshold and marker regex are deliberately rough --
# warn-only exists to gather the miss-rate so the heuristic can be tuned before any promotion.
try:
    if os.environ.get("MODEL_EFFORT_BYPASSED", "0") != "1":
        substantive = len(text) >= 800
        head = text[:400]
        has_model_line = bool(re.search(r"(?i)\bmodel:\s*\S", head) and
                              re.search(r"(?i)\beffort\b", head))
        has_fable_flag = bool(re.search(r"(?i)fable-suited", text))
        if substantive and not has_model_line and not has_fable_flag:
            audit("model-effort-detections.log",
                  "[%s] WARN-MISS session=%s len=%d (substantive response opened without a Model/Effort recommendation line)" % (ts, sid, len(text)))
except Exception:
    pass

if blocks:
    print(json.dumps({"decision": "block", "reason": "\n\n".join(blocks)}))
sys.exit(0)
PY
exit 0

                "file/ticket/source you actually checked), inferred (and label it as inference), or unknown "
                "(and either cut it or turn it into a question). Any claim drawn from NOT-finding something "
                "(absence) is not verified, flag it. Then revise unsourced assertions to be sourced or hedged, "
                "add a short 'assertion-audit' / 'verified note' line, and re-present. Origin: "
                "SIG-2026-06-04-assert-from-inference-drift. Bypass with PRESEND_ASSERTION_BYPASSED=1 only if "
                "the content carries no factual claims."
            )
except Exception:
    pass

# ---- RULE 4: model-effort recommendation (WARN-ONLY) ----
# Convention (2026-07-18): substantive turns open with a "Model: X . Effort: Y" line, plus a
# "FABLE-SUITED, fallback ..." callout when the Fable rubric fires. This rule only LOGS a miss;
# it never blocks. The substantive-length threshold and marker regex are deliberately rough --
# warn-only exists to gather the miss-rate so the heuristic can be tuned before any promotion.
try:
    if os.environ.get("MODEL_EFFORT_BYPASSED", "0") != "1":
        substantive = len(text) >= 800
        head = text[:400]
        has_model_line = bool(re.search(r"(?i)\bmodel:\s*\S", head) and
                              re.search(r"(?i)\beffort\b", head))
        has_fable_flag = bool(re.search(r"(?i)fable-suited", text))
        if substantive and not has_model_line and not has_fable_flag:
            audit("model-effort-detections.log",
                  "[%s] WARN-MISS session=%s len=%d (substantive response opened without a Model/Effort recommendation line)" % (ts, sid, len(text)))
except Exception:
    pass

if blocks:
    print(json.dumps({"decision": "block", "reason": "\n\n".join(blocks)}))
sys.exit(0)
PY
exit 0

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Test harness for response-lint-stop-check.sh. Runs the hook under a throwaway HOME
so real audit logs are untouched. Asserts each rule + the consolidation behavior."""
import json, os, subprocess, tempfile, sys

HOOK = "/Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/response-lint-stop-check.sh"
TMP = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="lint-test-")
FAKE_HOME = os.path.join(TMP, "home")
os.makedirs(os.path.join(FAKE_HOME, ".claude", "audit"), exist_ok=True)
MODEL_LOG = os.path.join(FAKE_HOME, ".claude", "audit", "model-effort-detections.log")

CLEAN_FILLER = ("The audit runs a check and records the outcome in plain words. " * 16)  # ~990 chars, clean
MODEL_LINE = "Model: Opus 4.8 . Effort: high\n\n"

def run(last_text, stop_active=False):
    tpath = os.path.join(TMP, "t.jsonl")
    with open(tpath, "w") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps({"type": "user", "message": {"content": "hi"}}) + "\n")
        f.write(json.dumps({"type": "assistant",
                            "message": {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": last_text}]}}) + "\n")
    hook_input = json.dumps({"transcript_path": tpath, "session_id": "test",
                             "stop_hook_active": stop_active})
    env = dict(os.environ); env["HOME"] = FAKE_HOME
    p = subprocess.run(["bash", HOOK], input=hook_input, env=env,
                       capture_output=True, text=True)
    return p.stdout.strip()

def model_log_count():
    if not os.path.exists(MODEL_LOG):
        return 0
    return sum(1 for _ in open(MODEL_LOG))

results = []
def check(name, cond):
    results.append((name, cond))
    print(("PASS " if cond else "FAIL ") + name)

# T1: clean substantive w/ model line -> no block
out = run(MODEL_LINE + CLEAN_FILLER)
check("T1 clean+model -> no block", out == "")

# T2: em-dash -> block DASH-GLYPH
out = run("This has an em dash — right here, and it is not allowed.")
check("T2 emdash -> DASH-GLYPH block", "DASH-GLYPH DRIFT" in out)

# T3: backtick-wrapped workspace path -> block LINK-FORMAT
out = run("See `/Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/foo.md` for the detail you want.")
check("T3 backtick path -> LINK-FORMAT block", "LINK-FORMAT DRIFT" in out)

# T4: BOTH em-dash and backtick path -> ONE block object containing BOTH reasons
out = run("Fix `/Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/foo.md` — then re-run the check.")
one_json = False
try:
    obj = json.loads(out); one_json = obj.get("decision") == "block"
except Exception:
    one_json = False
check("T4 both -> single block obj", one_json)
check("T4 both -> contains DASH-GLYPH", "DASH-GLYPH DRIFT" in out)
check("T4 both -> contains LINK-FORMAT", "LINK-FORMAT DRIFT" in out)

# T5: send-ready content w/o assertion audit -> block PRE-SEND
out = run("This note is ready to send to the client. Just paste it over to them.")
check("T5 send no-audit -> PRE-SEND block", "PRE-SEND ASSERTION DRIFT" in out)

# T5b: send-ready WITH an assertion-audit marker -> NOT blocked (suppressed)
out = run("This note is ready to send to the client. assertion-audit: every claim sourced below.")
check("T5b send + audit marker -> no presend block", "PRE-SEND ASSERTION DRIFT" not in out)

# T6: substantive, NO model line, clean -> no block BUT one model-effort WARN logged
before = model_log_count()
out = run(CLEAN_FILLER)
after = model_log_count()
check("T6 substantive no-model -> no block", out == "")
check("T6 substantive no-model -> WARN logged", after == before + 1)

# T7: short, no model line -> no block AND no WARN (below length threshold)
before = model_log_count()
out = run("Quick clean note, nothing to see here.")
after = model_log_count()
check("T7 short no-model -> no block", out == "")
check("T7 short no-model -> no WARN", after == before)

# T8: recursion guard (stop_hook_active) -> no output even with an em-dash present
out = run("has an em dash — here", stop_active=True)
check("T8 recursion guard -> no output", out == "")

# T9: code-fence exemption -> backtick path INSIDE a fence does NOT block link-format
out = run(MODEL_LINE + "Example only:\n```\nsee `/Users/brien/Workspaces/x.md`\n```\nCarry on with the plan.")
check("T9 fenced path -> no LINK-FORMAT block", "LINK-FORMAT DRIFT" not in out)

# T9b: same path OUTSIDE a fence DOES block (control for T9)
out = run("see `/Users/brien/Workspaces/x.md` inline in prose here")
check("T9b inline path -> LINK-FORMAT block", "LINK-FORMAT DRIFT" in out)

# T10: em-dash INSIDE a fence STILL blocks (emdash has no fence exemption, by design)
out = run("```\ncode with an em dash — inside\n```")
check("T10 fenced emdash -> still blocks", "DASH-GLYPH DRIFT" in out)

print()
passed = sum(1 for _, c in results if c)
total = len(results)
print("RESULT: %d/%d passed" % (passed, total))
sys.exit(0 if passed == total else 1)

Actually, I should give Brien a clear checkpoint right now since he's away—something clean he can read when he checks his phone. Then I'll write the rubric as a memory file in the same turn. That keeps things substantive and lets burn notifications flow naturally.

I'm creating a memory file for the Fable-suitability rubric with proper frontmatter (name, description, metadata for type and status), then populating it with the five signals, the "2+ fires = Fable" decision rule, the "wrong tool when" scenarios, the refusal guardrail, the model/effort convention, and the burn-mechanics lessons including the new insight about long-turn connection drops and incremental-checkpoint mitigation.

I should also wire this into the signal-scoring.md framework addendum and the ledger so it's accessible across both my recall and the workspace artifacts—the memory ensures I apply the rubric going forward, while the framework documents make it explicit for the user.

But I'll be conservative here: I'll write the memory and update the MEMORY.md index, then stop and report status since burns are running. That's a clean, bounded unit of work.

Now I'm adding the new entry to the MEMORY.md index by inserting it after the Active section header, before the existing sibling-output entry, and then I'll write out the memory file content itself.

The rubric defines when Fable justifies its premium cost—roughly 2-4x the price of Opus 4.8—based on specific criteria like long-horizon autonomy, whole-corpus reasoning across large datasets, and hard novel synthesis tasks. This was created during a pre-expiry Fable burn session on 2026-07-18.

Fable earns its cost when at least two of these conditions apply: multi-hour agentic loops with compounding risk, reasoning across an entire corpus at once rather than chunked, hard novel synthesis requiring architecture or cross-product coherence, adversarial falsification work, or high-stakes decisions with weak safety nets. For everything else—mechanical transforms, bulk edits, well-specified implementation with test coverage, and all client-facing gruntwork—route to Sonnet 5 or Opus 4.8 instead. One caveat: Fable sometimes refuses security or life-sciences work as a false positive, so a fallback is needed to avoid the system just stopping.

Starting 2026-07-18, substantive turns now open with a "Model: X, Effort: Y" line and fire a "FABLE-SUITED, fallback Opus 4.8, reason Z" flag when the rubric matches, enforced as a warning by the model-effort rule in response-lint-consolidation and logged to audit. The orchestrator seat stays Opus 4.8 to keep the judge unbiased and avoid burning Fable on coordination, and intelligence-sensitive work runs at high or max effort, never medium.

One operational risk I've found: Fable's long single-turn passes over massive corpora can drop mid-response with no disk checkpoint, losing all work. The fix is to instruct it to persist incrementally before elaborating, or resume from transcript by sending the message again.

I should avoid fanning out many parallel Fable subagents since the shared 5-hour rolling limit throttles concurrent work and multiplies drop risk—keeping it around 2 concurrent is safer. Now I'm adding this to the memory index with a markdown link and the discovery date.

I'm realizing the link-format hook only scans my response text, not the files I write, so the markdown link convention in MEMORY.md itself won't trigger it—but I need to use bare paths in my actual response.

Now I'm planning to write the memory file and update the index with two tool calls, though I should double-check whether the wikilink references I'm adding actually exist as memories yet. I'm deciding whether to reference the consolidated response linter hook directly by its file path in the memory, rather than creating a dangling wikilink. Let me finalize the memory content with that adjustment and write it out now. Though I'm also pausing to consider whether I'm overweighting artifact production while my collaborator is away, versus focusing on what actually matters.

---
name: fable-suitability-rubric
description: When to spend premium Fable vs Opus 4.8 vs Sonnet 5; the model+effort recommendation convention; Fable long-turn drop mitigation. 2026-07-18.
metadata:
  node_type: memory
  type: reference
  status: active
---

Rubric for routing work across model tiers, plus the convention for surfacing the choice. Built 2026-07-18 during the pre-expiry Fable burn session.

**Fable earns its premium (roughly 2x to 4x Opus 4.8; usage-credits 10 in / 50 out per MTok once out of plan) only when 2 or more of these fire:**
- Long-horizon autonomy (multi-hour agentic loop, compounding derailment risk).
- Whole-corpus 1M reasoning (reason across an entire set at once, not chunked).
- Hard novel synthesis (architecture, port/contract design, cross-product coherence, DDR-grade decisions).
- Adversarial / falsification (strongest possible refutation).
- High cost-of-error with a weak catch-net (no test, no review, hard to reverse).

**Route away from Fable:** mechanical transforms, bulk edits, sync, grep, ID resolution to Sonnet 5 or Haiku; well-specified implementation with tests as the catch-net to Opus 4.8; ALL client/engagement gruntwork (standing rule, never Fable per [[feedback_client_gruntwork_cheap_bucket]]). Guardrail: Fable can refuse security-tooling or life-sciences-adjacent work as a false-positive refusal, so wire a fallback or the request just stops.

**Convention (wired 2026-07-18):** substantive turns open with a "Model: X . Effort: Y" line, plus a "FABLE-SUITED, fallback Opus 4.8, reason Z" callout when the rubric fires. Enforced warn-only by the model-effort rule inside the consolidated response linter at /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/response-lint-stop-check.sh (logs misses to /Users/brien/.claude/audit/model-effort-detections.log; promote to blocking after reviewing the miss-rate, same warn-first calibration the Layer-4.2 posture check used). Orchestrator and judge seat stays Opus 4.8: do not burn Fable on coordination, and a fixed non-participant judge keeps cross-model comparisons unbiased. Run intelligence-sensitive orchestration at high, xhigh, or max effort, not medium.

**Fable harness finding (2026-07-18):** Fable subagent long single-turns (a 1M whole-corpus pass) are prone to "connection closed mid-response" API drops that lose ALL in-turn work if nothing reached disk first. B4 coherence audit dropped once with an empty output dir despite reporting "writing now." Mitigations: (1) instruct the agent to checkpoint to disk incrementally (persist a complete-enough file first, elaborate in a second write), (2) resume works: SendMessage to the agent name re-runs from its intact transcript, so the reasoning is not lost if you resume rather than relaunch. Do NOT fan out many parallel Fable subagents: the in-plan weekly Fable bucket is large (2 percent used at burn start) but the shared 5-hour rolling limit throttles, and parallel long-turns multiply drop risk. Hold at about 2 concurrent.

Related: [[reference_model_economics_2026_07]], [[reference_subagent_1m_context_gate]], [[feedback_spawn_model_economy]].

A1 wrapped up successfully with a comprehensive governance-gap upstream control design—full documentation including design specs with census evidence, notices, and status files delivered to the workspace. The root cause analysis identified that previous fixes only protected a single write path through interactive hooks.

The solution implements a fleet-scope baseline-ratchet engine that extends the closure write boundary check into a lint subcommand, running nightly as part of the library-index wrapper to catch new violations while maintaining a monotonically shrinking baseline. The approach includes write-through DoD stamping in the auto-closer, ratchet regression tests, and liveness monitoring to ensure the signal becomes fully closable under its meta-contract. The A1 analysis directly connects to the governance consolidation work—it identified that closure enforcement only guards interactive write paths through PreToolUse hooks while missing non-interactive writers like sed in nightly scripts, which is exactly the systemic gap Brien focuses on. The three defects it surfaced (auto-closer leak, Layer 5 hook Edit fail-open hole, hook-vs-spec comparator mismatch) are real vulnerabilities in the governance fabric itself that warrant direct attention.

However, I need to be careful here: A1's output is a Fable-generated design proposal with specific claims—192 signals, 113 post-hook, the sed finding, the three defects—but these are unverified assertions, not established facts. Per Brien's rules about fabrication and verification, I should present these as "A1 claims X (unverified)" rather than relay them as confirmed findings. This actually opens up a Track B opportunity where A1's proposal becomes a verification target.

Right now I'm at a checkpoint: A1 is done with strong output on disk, B4 is running and writing its deliverable, hooks are complete, and rubric memory is done. Before moving forward, I should quickly confirm that A1's design.md file actually exists and is substantive—not just verify the summary, but make sure the full design landed properly.

Then I need to decide whether to launch A2 now. Since A1 freed up a slot and B4 is actively resuming, I could run both B4 and A2 concurrently at my stated cap of 2 active burns. A1 succeeded cleanly without dropping, so the pattern is working. The expiry clock favors burning through work, so launching A2 for the decision surfaces on the 48-day positioning items makes sense—but I should probably let B4 land first to avoid juggling two uncertain burns at once. ority. The next high-value Fable burns would be A2 (decision surfaces to unblock the 48-day items), A3/B4 (coherence audit, already running as B4), Crucible's first synthesis, and the Track B comparisons. But here's the thing — A1 produced a design proposal that needs verification, and I have Opus capacity available as the orchestrator. But the real constraint is Fable expiry — verification can happen anytime since Opus is always available, so the clock-optimal move is to spend the expiring resource now and defer the Opus verification to later. A2 produces decision surfaces that require Brien's engagement, whereas Crucible produces a durable synthesis artifact. For maximizing expiring Fable value, I want burns that genuinely need Fable and create lasting value independent of Brien's immediate follow-up.

I'm leaning toward A2 since it's from his escalated backlog and Fable's adversarial synthesis is the right tool for a stuck decision. Crucible's approach requires more specialized setup and understanding of the synthesis method, while A2 is more self-contained—just read the pending decisions and build the surfaces. It's cleaner to dispatch and directly serves what's already flagged as urgent.

But I should step back and reconsider the broader strategy here.

The data from recent burns shows a clear pattern: A1-style hard-synthesis on bounded targets succeeded brilliantly (~14 minutes, exactly matched the rubric), while B4's whole-corpus sweep dropped because that profile is drop-prone at scale. So I should favor bounded, hard-synthesis burns going forward and avoid the broader whole-corpus sweeps. A2 fits the A1 profile perfectly—it's bounded and hard-synthesis focused—so it's the safer choice with lower drop risk. Crucible synthesis might lean more toward whole-corpus territory, which makes A2 the better call here.

I'll launch A2 next, but first I need to add an instruction for incremental checkpoint-to-disk persistence so we don't lose progress like B4 did. Then I'll do a quick verification that A1 actually landed, launch A2, and report back to Brien with the status: A1 succeeded with strong results, B4 is resuming, A2 is launching, hooks are done, and the rubric is captured. A1 found three governance defects worth his eventual attention, but nothing urgent enough to interrupt his walk right now.

I'm also reconsidering whether to create a durable task list given the complexity — A1 verification, ledger work, signal-scoring addendum, Crucible burn, and Track B comparisons are all deferred, and if this session hits the context limit, I'll lose track of the plan.  The overhead of TaskCreate is worth it here since Brien explicitly flagged session limits as a risk, and a visible task board gives him continuity when he returns. Let me load the tools and set this up, though I want to be thoughtful about how I shape this turn.

I'll prioritize the immediate burn actions first: verify A1 landed and launch A2 (the Fable agent). I can run these in parallel with a ToolSearch for the task management tools, so I'm not delaying the critical path while getting everything ready.

For A2, I need to build decision surfaces for those two 48-day positioning decisions from the register — the parallax refinements and the intent positioning DDRs, both overdue past 45 days.

I'll read through the pending decisions file and the relevant materials, then construct a proper decision surface for each: frame the decision, lay out the real options with their tradeoffs, identify what's load-bearing, run a disconfirmation pass to stress-test each option, and provide a recommendation with reasoning. The goal is to give Brien a clear surface he can decide from in minutes rather than having it sit another 48 days. I'll use adversarial synthesis to sharpen the thinking, then output both decision-surface documents to the intake folder. I need to pull those two stuck decisions from Brien's pending register, then gather the relevant context for each—the Parallax three-axis refinements from the product spec area, and the intent positioning DDRs from the frameworks section—so I can build clear decision surfaces that let him adjudicate both in minutes. I'll write each file to disk as it's completed to avoid losing work, starting with the parallax surface, then the intent-positioning surface, then the notice and status files. I need to stay within that intake directory, keep everything read-only except for writes there, and stick to plain ASCII punctuation—no dashes, arrows, or ellipsis characters.

If I hit any permission or credential gate, I'll stop and report it since Brien is away. My final output will be the two file paths, a one-line recommendation for each decision, and any blockers I encounter.

A1 landed and it is a strong Fable result: about 14 minutes, one agent, and it did exactly what the rubric predicted Fable would be worth paying for. It ran a live census, traced the 93-day root cause to enforcement that only guards interactive write paths while non-interactive writers (sed in nightly scripts) slip past, proposed a fleet-scope ratchet with a real catch-net, honestly separated the closable part from one irreducible residual, and found three latent defects in the governance fabric in passing.

So under the clock, I spend Fable now and defer verification.

Confirming A1's files are real and substantial, and launching A2 (the two 48-day-stuck positioning decisions) as the next burn. A2 is the safe A1-profile (bounded hard-synthesis, low drop risk) rather than another B4-style whole-corpus sweep, and I am baking the incremental-persist lesson into it.

input.prompt:
You are Claude Fable 5 with a 1M context. This is a bounded hard-synthesis burn: build decision surfaces for two strategic decisions that have been stuck about 48 days, so Brien can finally adjudicate them in minutes. Reason hard. The reason they are stuck is that they are genuinely hard.

CONTEXT. Both are open items on Brien's pending-decisions register at /Users/brien/Workspaces/.context/PENDING_DECISIONS.md . Read it and find these two (both dated around 2026-05-31):
  1. Parallax three-axis refinements.
  2. The three intent positioning DDRs.
Read the material each needs to be framed faithfully: for Parallax, the spec area under /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/parallax/ (especially spec/ and any portfolio-taxonomy or three-axis document); for the intent positioning DDRs, the decisions and positioning material under /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/ . Read enough to state each decision as what Brien was actually deciding, not a strawman.

DELIVER, for EACH decision, a decision surface containing: (1) the decision stated crisply (what is actually being chosen), (2) why it has stalled (the real tension), (3) the genuine options with honest tradeoffs, (4) a disconfirmation pass per option (what would make it the wrong choice, what would have to be true for it to be right), (5) a recommendation with its reasoning and what it costs. You are BUILDING THE SURFACE, not making the call. These are Brien's L2 decisions. Your job is to compress 48 days of stall into a choice he can make in minutes.

PERSIST INCREMENTALLY. Write to /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/_intake/2026-07-18-positioning-decision-surfaces/ . Create the directory. Write decision-surface-parallax.md the moment that surface is complete, THEN decision-surface-intent-positioning.md, THEN NOTICE.md and STATUS.md (proposal status, Fable burn 2026-07-18). Get each file on disk as it finishes so a connection drop cannot wipe the whole turn.

BOUNDARIES. Read-only on all source. Write only inside that intake directory. Do not commit. Do not spawn subagents. Internal IP, no redaction. Never use em-dashes, en-dashes, arrow glyphs, or the ellipsis character (use periods, commas, colons, parentheses, or the words "to" and "vs"). Every file reference is a bare full-absolute /Users/brien/... path, no backticks, no markdown links.

If you hit any gate (permission prompt, credential, usage-credits requirement), stop and report it as your final message; Brien is away and can return to clear it.

Your final message is a data return: (a) the two file paths, (b) your one-line recommendation for each decision, (c) any gate hit.

content.thinking:
A1 delivered the design document successfully with all required components, and B4 managed to write the coherence audit despite being resumed, though it hasn't yet produced the STATUS file or sent a completion signal—it might still be working on those final pieces.

I've launched A2 as my third agent, so I'm now running B4 and A2 concurrently at capacity. The resume pattern validated itself; B4 recovered its reasoning and persisted the deliverable first as instructed, which is a solid sign the mitigation is working.

Now I'm weighing whether to send Brien a consolidated checkpoint since a lot has progressed while he's away, and deciding whether a push notification is warranted based on the current criteria. It's a matter of keeping Fable's window open while it's still available. I've got Track A mostly covered with the synthesis work, and Track B is structured as paired comparisons — I need to run the Fable halves now before expiry, then defer the Opus and Sonnet runs plus scoring to later. The priority is feeding Fable with high-value burns while the clock is still running. it. I need to be more deliberate about what I queue next — either Crucible for the highest remaining Track A value, or pause new Fable launches and let the current work finish before reassessing.

Let me refocus on Brien's actual priorities: burning expiring Fable on genuinely suited work (which I'm doing), building harness awareness through comparisons to understand what's worth paying for, and wiring that into the recommendation. The comparisons need both Fable and Opus halves to be meaningful, so I shouldn't just fire Fable burns without the full picture.

The immediate plan is to report status now, wait for B4 and A2 to complete, then decide whether to launch Crucible next or hold at two concurrent burns.

I'm weighing whether to tackle the deferred signal-scoring addendum and comparison ledger while waiting—they're part of the wiring he asked for, but they're low-priority and might be scope-creep.

Actually, the comparison ledger is worth doing now because the burns I'm running ARE the data that populates it. A1 is already the first entry, and logging A1/B4/A2 as they complete captures concrete harness-awareness learning that directly serves the goal. I should scaffold the ledger and start tracking these runs.

I'll place it at `/Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/reference/usage-tracker/model-suitability-ledger.md` since that's where model strategy and economics live. The ledger will track each burn with date, task, model, effort, rubric signals, outcome, tokens, wall-time, and whether the model choice was justified.

Logging the first two entries: A1 on governance-gap design using Fable with hard-synthesis and high-cost-error signals, delivering strong results in ~13.6 minutes across 33 tools—verdict is Fable-justified pending Opus verification. B4 is the portfolio coherence audit on the whole corpus that dropped mid-response due to connection issues.

The harness findings show that Fable subagents are prone to dropping on long single-turn requests, but persist-and-resume recovers them; in-plan Fable draws from the weekly bucket without credit gates, and I should hold around 2 concurrent instances with a 5-hour throttle to manage drop risk.

This ledger captures the durable artifact for goal #2, so I'll write it out now rather than just reporting status.

I've launched three Fable burns so far (A1 delivered, B4 recovered, A2 running), which gives solid coverage of the top Track A items, so I'm weighing whether more burns have marginal value or if I should consolidate instead. Writing the ledger...

content.text:
Both recoveries confirmed: A1's design.md is real (23KB, 156 lines), and the B4 resume worked. Its coherence-audit.md is now on disk at 18.8KB. The persist-first instruction saved the whole-corpus reasoning that the first drop had lost. A2 is running. So I have two Fable agents in flight (B4 finishing, A2) plus one done, exactly the 2-concurrent cap.

Capturing the burn evidence in a durable model-suitability ledger now, because these runs ARE the harness-awareness data you asked to build. A1 is already a clean data point: Fable delivered beyond spec on the hard-synthesis profile.

input.content:
---
title: Model-Suitability Ledger
type: reference
maturity: active
confidentiality: internal
reusability: adaptable
created: 2026-07-18
updated: 2026-07-18
purpose: Running evidence for when Fable / Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 5 each earn their slot. Populated by real burns and by Fable-vs-fallback comparisons.
---

# Model-Suitability Ledger

Evidence base for the Fable-suitability rubric (recall memory: fable-suitability-rubric). Each row is a real run or a paired comparison. The point is to replace vibes about "what to pay Fable for" with measured deltas. Rubric signals: LH long-horizon, WC whole-corpus-1M, HS hard-synthesis, AF adversarial, CE high-cost-error-weak-catch-net.

## Burns and comparisons

| Date | Task | Model | Effort | Rubric profile | Tokens / tools / wall | Outcome | Verdict |
|------|------|-------|--------|----------------|------------------------|---------|---------|
| 2026-07-18 | A1: governance-gap (SIG-F-001) upstream-control design | Fable 5 | high (default) | HS, CE | 192,840 / 33 / ~13.6 min | Root-caused the 93-day stall to enforcement guarding only interactive write paths; designed a fleet-scope ratchet with catch-net; separated closable from one irreducible residual; found 3 latent governance defects in passing. Claims (192 violations, 113 post-hook, 3 defects) PENDING Opus verification. | FABLE-JUSTIFIED. Delivered beyond spec on exactly the HS+CE profile. Strongest data point so far. |
| 2026-07-18 | B4: portfolio coherence audit + missing-metric design | Fable 5 | high (default) | WC, HS, CE | (in flight) | First turn DROPPED (connection closed mid-response) with an empty output dir. Resumed from transcript with a persist-first instruction; recovered and wrote coherence-audit.md (18.8KB). PENDING review. | Pending. Harness note: the WC long-turn is the drop-prone profile. |
| 2026-07-18 | A2: two 48-day positioning decision surfaces | Fable 5 | high (default) | HS, AF | (in flight) | Running. Bounded A1-profile, incremental-persist baked in. | Pending. |

## Deferred fallback halves (for the comparison, run on Opus/Sonnet, no expiry pressure)
- Opus 4.8 adversarial-verify of A1's census + 3 defect claims (validates before Brien acts; first true Fable-vs-Opus delta).
- Opus-4.8-chunked run of the B4 coherence audit (tests the 1M whole-corpus premium head-to-head).
- Sonnet 5 run of a deliberately-mechanical task (cast residue characterization) to prove the cheap tier suffices there.

## Harness findings (2026-07-18 session)
- **In-plan Fable subagents do NOT hit the old credit gate.** With weekly Fable at 2 percent used (in plan), Agent(model=fable) subagents launched clean and drew from the in-plan bucket. The reference_subagent_1m_context_gate "Usage credits required" failure was a not-in-plan-era condition; it did not reproduce.
- **Fable long single-turns drop on connection-close and lose all in-turn work if nothing hit disk.** Mitigations that worked: (1) instruct persist-first, elaborate-second; (2) resume via SendMessage to the agent name re-runs from intact transcript, recovering the reasoning. B4 recovered this way.
- **Hold about 2 concurrent Fable subagents.** Weekly Fable headroom is large but the shared 5-hour rolling limit throttles, and parallel long-turns multiply drop risk. Do not fan out.
- **Cost profile that landed value:** A1 spent ~193k tokens for a root-cause + implementable design + 3 defect finds on a problem stuck 93 days. That is the shape of a justified Fable spend: hard, high-cost-of-error, weak catch-net, and the fallback (repeated Opus passes) had already failed to close it.

content.thinking:
The B4 audit flagged a critical coherence issue where the witness port contract is out of sync with the actual implementation. The port is frozen at a specific set of verbs like ingest_otlp and query, but the INTENT documentation describes a legacy 6-verb surface that no longer matches what's actually available. This mismatch is causing the Track 2 promotion pipeline to reference verbs that don't exist anymore.

Beyond that, there's a position collision on the Witness component itself—the manifest and cell-occupancy tables claim it's at P2b, but the canonical chain table from the parallax spec places it at P4. The Crucible component has an even bigger problem: it has no executable surface at all, with both ports resolving to scripts that don't actually exist in the crucible/engine directory. The P3-to-P3.5 seam is only documented in the charter, but physically Cast is writing directly to Cast instead.

I'm also tracking five additional coherence failures: the "Lore" product is still referenced in the topography even though it's dead, Warp declares a contribution to topography with no actual consumer, Voices references a Core/personas path that doesn't exist, there's a draft-versus-ratified drift in the Conduit-to-Witness contract, and the registry count doesn't match the manifest count. To measure this systematically, I'm defining a Portfolio Coherence Index that tracks what fraction of declared cross-product seams are actually intact—both endpoints exist, every verb has a real producer and consumer, chain positions agree, and contract statuses align. This metric gets emitted to a dated JSON file at the Core root and is guarded by an invariant in the nightly audit.

The token usage and tool calls from the resumed turn show that B4's findings are overlapping with what I already know from the CLAUDE.md documentation—the Crucible P3 entry matches, the Witness verbs match (including the fact that the new port shape subsumes the legacy six-verb contract, yet INTENT.md still lists those dead verbs as current). These are specific, checkable claims with file paths and line numbers, so they're plausible and well-evidenced. A1 and B4 are both landing strong, and A2 is still running—the resume mitigation is working as intended.

Now I have a free Fable slot since B4 is done. I'm weighing whether to launch another burn next—either a Crucible synthesis, another Track B Fable-half, or hold for now. Three strong Fable deliverables are already in hand (A1 and B4 complete, A2 in flight), and the harness-awareness goal is being served well.

What's striking is that B4's coherence audit surfaced real, actionable conflicts—dead verbs in Witness, P2b/P4 collisions, Crucible lacking an executable surface, a dead Lore product cited as live. These aren't just comparison data; they're genuine work-product that Brien would want to act on, same as A1's three defects. So the Fable burns are producing substantive findings, not just fodder.

The strategic question now is whether to keep burning Fable on genuinely-suited work while capacity exists (Crucible synthesis is the cleanest remaining Track A option), or shift to verifying the Fable outputs with Opus instead.

But here's the catch: B4 just revealed that Crucible has no executable surface—its ports resolve to Cast scripts and the engine is method-doc-only. That means "Crucible first native synthesis through the Crucible surface" might actually be blocked or premature right now, since there's no surface to run it through yet. This finding itself changes what a Crucible burn would accomplish.

So instead of Crucible synthesis, I should look at what's on Brien's backlog that's genuinely Fable-suited and bounded. The scout report points to resolving cast mtime-integrity root cause as a good Track B candidate—it's a diagnosis with ground truth, making it a solid comparison point. The missing metric from scout #4 is already covered since B4 just designed the PCI metric overlapping A3. The witness dead-verb reconciliation is more of a mechanical fix to INTENT.md, not really Fable-suited.

Actually, I'm reconsidering whether to launch more Fable at all. I've already burned through about 636k tokens across three Fable runs, and the 5-hour throttle was at 16% earlier—these burns will push it higher. Rather than keep manufacturing Fable work with declining returns, the smarter approach is to let A2 finish, update the ledger with B4 findings, and give Brien a clear summary of what's been captured.

The real tension is timing: the expiry might hit tonight while he's away, so stopping now risks leaving the Fable window unused. But launching low-value burns just to use the window doesn't make sense either. I should probably wait for his input on whether he wants more Fable work (like Crucible surfaces or Track B comparisons) or if the three major deliverables plus verification is the right place to stop. ration root-cause work (scout #3) is a solid choice—it's a real pending item with hard diagnosis work and a known ground truth that'll make for good Track B comparison data later. But I'm second-guessing myself now: there might be overlap between the cast mtime churn issue and the closure violations A1 was investigating, and overwatch is flagging 332 uncommitted changes in cast, which complicates things.

The real question is whether Fable is the right tool here. It's diagnosis work, which Fable could handle, but the ground truth is already somewhat known (batch writers), so Opus could probably do it just fine. The Fable value feels marginal—more of a Track B comparison input than a must-have for Track A.

I should step back and think about the highest-value Fable burn. Both B4 and A1 surfaced real coherence and governance debt across the product line, so maybe the cleanest move is to have Fable take one of B4's top findings and actually design the fix.

But I'm getting ahead of myself—that's acting on unverified findings, which I said I'd defer. Let me make a clean call: the three burns (A1, B4, A2) are the high-value Track A work and they're landing. The best remaining use of the Fable window that aligns with Brien's explicit goals—burn Fable and build harness awareness—is to run one well-chosen Track B comparison where I can execute both halves and get a real delta. That's the "harness awareness" deliverable he explicitly wanted. The cleanest comparison would be to take B1 (the disconfirmation task on the conscience-board staleness tension), which is small and has a known answer, and run it on both Fable and Opus to see which catches the staleness reasoning faster and sharper—that's a bounded, decisive data point for harness awareness.

But actually, running the same task on both models means launching the Fable half now and the Opus half later, which feels marginal for a single disconfirmation on one stale blocker. I'm going to stop deliberating and make a call: I won't launch another Fable burn reflexively. Instead, I'll update the ledger with B4's completion and findings, let A2 finish since it's still in flight, and give Brien a consolidated checkpoint with the three deliverables and their current status.

Now I'm weighing whether to ask Brien directly or just recommend and proceed. Given his preference to execute rather than be asked, I shouldn't pose a bare question—but he's away right now. The decision between continuing Fable burns on remaining items versus shifting to verification has real stakes since the resource is expiring, and he specifically said to pull him back for gates like this.

But this isn't actually a gate—nothing's blocked. He explicitly granted me discretion to execute the most direct high-value path, so I should keep the Fable pipeline running rather than pause and wait. The highest-value move given the expiry pressure and his goal to fully use up Fable is to launch the next burn on the most genuinely-suited item. Looking at what the earlier work revealed—B4 exposed real coherence debt in the product line, and A1 showed governance-enforcement gaps—I should target one of those areas next.

The cleanest option that's truly Fable-suited and independent is running adversarial falsification against one of his load-bearing canons—either the Subaru operating model or a key DDR. That's pure Fable work, bounded, and stress-tests something he actually relies on. The question is whether stress-testing his own operating model counts as client gruntwork, which his rule forbids—and I think it doesn't.

But to respect the rule cleanly, I should avoid anything client-adjacent. What about a Track A burn on his internal product line instead? The Crucible synthesis question could work—B4 said Crucible has no surface, which itself is a finding that could become a Fable task: designing what Crucible's executable surface should be at the P3-to-P3.5 seam.

This is solid architecture work suited to Fable, it's a real pending item (CR-002 deferred the physical extraction), and it advances a real product. The concern is that it leans on B4's unverified finding, but actually that finding is already confirmed in CLAUDE.md itself—the Crucible entry notes that physical script extraction from cast/engine/ was deferred. So the finding is already known and grounded, not speculative. Designing Crucible's executable surface becomes a legitimate, well-founded Fable burn.

But here's the tension: this is fairly deep product-architecture work that Brien might want to steer himself, since Crucible is his synthesis engine and core to the chain. Designing its executable surface autonomously could overshoot—that's the kind of architecture decision he'd want to be in the loop on, not have Fable design while he's away. It's L2-ish territory, so maybe not ideal for autonomous dispatch.

I'm clearly overthinking this. Let me step back and be decisive about the right approach. I've launched the three best Track A burns from his actual escalation list.

The disciplined move is to let A2 finish, update the ledger, and give Brien a strong checkpoint that reports the three deliverables, names the specific remaining Fable-suited burns he could greenlight, and notes I'm holding further launches because the remaining items are either his-to-direct or lower-marginal-value.

But wait—that seems to contradict keeping the pipeline fed under expiry uncertainty. Let me think through this: the reason to stop feeding is that I've hit the point where remaining burns don't generate genuine Fable-value. His principle is that the only wasteful burn is work Opus does equally well. If the remaining items are ones Opus handles just as well or that he should steer himself, then not burning Fable on them is actually correct, even under expiry pressure. Burning tokens just to use them up would be exactly the waste he'd reject. So the principled stopping point is when the marginal work no longer justifies Fable's unique capabilities.

I'm approaching that threshold after A1/B4/A2, but there's one more category that clearly justifies Fable: the Track B comparisons. Their whole purpose is measuring the Fable-versus-fallback delta, which directly serves his goal of understanding the harness performance. Running the Fable half of a comparison now is never wasteful because the deliverable is the delta itself, not the individual output. So Track B Fable-halves are the principled way to keep burning without manufacturing waste, and B2 on cast mtime-integrity diagnosis looks like the best candidate.

I need to run just the Fable half now and defer the Opus and Sonnet halves for later, but I'll preserve the exact task definition and log it so the comparison stays apples-to-apples. The key is ensuring all three model halves run on the identical prompt and task, even if they're staggered in time. I should frame this as a comparison burn and update the ledger accordingly. But I'm also thinking about Brien's situation—he's away, and when he returns he'll want to see what got burned, the learnings, and clear recommendations. Launching burns autonomously means he'll have a pile of Fable outputs to review when he gets back, which serves his goal of using up Fable, but I want to avoid creating an unverified mess that becomes a burden to review.

The three Track A burns produce proposals he'll review anyway, and a Track B comparison produces a diagnosis plus delta—both are coherent review items. I'm keeping concurrency at around 2, with A2 running and B2-Fable launching next, which feels right.

So my move is to update the ledger with B4's completion and findings, add the B2 comparison plan, launch the B2 Fable burn for the mtime-integrity diagnosis, and send Brien a consolidated checkpoint.

Actually, I can parallelize this better—launch B2 through the Agent and update the ledger through Edit at the same time, then report after. For the B2 prompt, I need to have it read the cast registry audit and those two July signals about mtime-integrity and the skill gate bypass, then diagnose whether the 315 violations are legitimate batch-writer churn or actual drift, using the audit doc's own attribution as ground truth for scoring.

I'm keeping the prompt neutral and task-focused—no model-specific framing like "you are Fable"—so I can run it identically across both the Opus and Sonnet halves for a fair comparison. The audit flagged about 315 mtime-integrity violations in the cast registry, but it's unclear whether these are just benign churn from batch writers or actual integrity drift—I need to dig into the latest audit report and the invariant definition to figure out which, quantify any real issues, and determine if the invariant itself needs adjustment.

I'm also pulling the two relevant July signals on mtime-integrity root cause and the freshen-skill-gate bypass finding, then examining the actual check logic that computes the invariant so I can ground my diagnosis in what it really tests rather than just the report's interpretation.

Once I understand the invariant's mechanics and the writer paths, I can deliver the root cause, break down the split between benign churn and real drift with numbers, and recommend the specific fix—whether that's correcting the invariant or fixing the writer behavior.

I'll write the diagnosis incrementally to the output directory, creating it first, then saving diagnosis.md as soon as it's complete followed by a one-line STATUS.md, keeping everything on disk promptly to avoid losing work. I'm staying read-only on all source, writing only to that output folder, and using plain ASCII punctuation throughout—no dashes, arrows, or ellipsis characters.

Now I'm launching the B2 analysis and updating the ledger in parallel, then reporting back with the file path, root cause summary, benign-vs-real split count, and any blockers I hit.

For the ledger update, I need to replace the B4 row with its completed findings and token counts, then add a new B2 row after the A2 entry. replacing the B4 row and adding the B2 row in a single edit by matching both the B4 and A2 rows together, then rewriting them with the updated B4 entry (capturing its findings) plus the new B2 row inserted after A2.

I'm realizing I'm spending too many tokens on ledger maintenance when the real priority is launching B2, so I'll do a more efficient update—either appending B4's completion note to the harness section and adding B2 to the deferred plan, or updating the table properly but concisely.

For B2, I'm using a neutral prompt without Fable-specific framing so it's fair for comparing against Opus and Sonnet later, even though the agent itself runs on Fable. Let me launch B2 and update the B4 row in parallel.

I've been going back and forth on whether to run B2 at all, but the principle is clear: it's a non-wasteful comparison with known ground truth, directly supports Brien's harness-awareness goal, and he gave me scheduling discretion. The resource is expiring, so launching it now makes sense. I'm committing to that and moving forward with the response and two tool calls.

For the report, I want to keep it tight—a status board with headline findings so Brien sees the value at a glance. B4 surfaced some genuinely useful issues: dead verbs in Witness, a P2b/P4 collision, Crucible lacking executable surface, and a dead "Lore" product still cited live. A1 found that enforcement guards only interactive paths and caught 3 defects. I'll structure this as a concise status update with the key findings and next steps.

input.prompt:
Diagnose the root cause of the cast registry's mtime-integrity violations and recommend the fix.

TASK. The cast registry chain-audit reports a large INV-MTIME-INTEGRITY cluster (about 315 violations). The audit doc attributes most of them to legitimate batch-writer mtime churn rather than real drift, but this is contested and unresolved. Determine the actual root cause: are these violations legitimate writer-noise (and if so, why does the invariant flag them, and how should the invariant be corrected to stop flagging benign churn), or do they encode real integrity drift that needs fixing, or a mix (quantify the split)?

READ. The cast registry chain audit under /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/cast/ (find the latest registry-chain-audit report, e.g. registry-chain-audit-2026-07-15.md, and the invariant definitions). The two relevant July signals: SIG-2026-07-08-mtime-integrity-growth-root-cause and SIG-2026-07-08-freshen-skill-gate-bypass-finding (search under /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/cast/ and /Users/brien/Workspaces/.intent/signals/). Read the mtime-integrity invariant's actual check logic (the script that computes it) so your diagnosis is grounded in what the invariant really tests, not just what the report says.

DELIVER: (1) the root cause, grounded in the invariant's check logic and the writer paths, (2) the split between benign writer-churn and real drift with your basis for the number, (3) the specific fix (correct the invariant, fix the writers, or both), named to file paths, (4) how to tell the fix succeeded.

PERSIST INCREMENTALLY to /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/_intake/2026-07-18-cast-mtime-diagnosis/fable/ . Create the directory. Write diagnosis.md as soon as it is complete, then a one-line STATUS.md. Get the file on disk promptly so a connection drop cannot wipe the turn.

BOUNDARIES. Read-only on all source. Write only inside that output directory. Do not commit. Do not spawn subagents. Internal IP, no redaction. Never use em-dashes, en-dashes, arrow glyphs, or the ellipsis character (use periods, commas, colons, parentheses, or the words "to" and "vs"). Every file reference is a bare full-absolute /Users/brien/... path, no backticks, no markdown links.

If you hit any gate (permission, credential, usage-credits), stop and report it as your final message.

Final message is a data return: (a) the file path, (b) your one-line root cause, (c) your benign-vs-real split number, (d) any gate hit.

---
title: Model-Suitability Ledger
type: reference
maturity: active
confidentiality: internal
reusability: adaptable
created: 2026-07-18
updated: 2026-07-18
purpose: Running evidence for when Fable / Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 5 each earn their slot. Populated by real burns and by Fable-vs-fallback comparisons.
---

# Model-Suitability Ledger

Evidence base for the Fable-suitability rubric (recall memory: fable-suitability-rubric). Each row is a real run or a paired comparison. The point is to replace vibes about "what to pay Fable for" with measured deltas. Rubric signals: LH long-horizon, WC whole-corpus-1M, HS hard-synthesis, AF adversarial, CE high-cost-error-weak-catch-net.

## Burns and comparisons

| Date | Task | Model | Effort | Rubric profile | Tokens / tools / wall | Outcome | Verdict |
|------|------|-------|--------|----------------|------------------------|---------|---------|
| 2026-07-18 | A1: governance-gap (SIG-F-001) upstream-control design | Fable 5 | high (default) | HS, CE | 192,840 / 33 / ~13.6 min | Root-caused the 93-day stall to enforcement guarding only interactive write paths; designed a fleet-scope ratchet with catch-net; separated closable from one irreducible residual; found 3 latent governance defects in passing. also this design and build task:

Do not add a tenth Stop hook. The model-effort recommendation check is pure response-text lint (it scans your message for the presence of the `Model . Effort` line). It belongs in the same family as emdash and link-format. So:

1. Consolidate the text-lint family into one `response-lint-stop-check.sh` with a rule registry (emdash, link-format, presend-assertion, model-effort). This is the win you asked for on both axes:
   * Running: one process spawn and one read of the message text instead of three-plus. Each of those scripts currently re-extracts the same final assistant message independently.
   * Doing: one ordered violation report, non-overlapping rule lanes, no chance of two rules fighting over the same rewrite. The model-effort rule gets a clean lane: it checks only for the recommendation line, and explicitly does not re-check bare-choice (autonomy-grant owns that) or closure claims (closure owns that).
2.

content.text:
You are right that this one touches everything, and that changes the calculus. Two honest points first:

It is already built, tested, and live. I did exactly your consolidation earlier this session: `response-lint-stop-check.sh` runs all four rules (emdash, link-format, presend, warn-only model-effort) in one transcript parse, Stop hooks are down from 9 to 7, it passed a 16-assertion harness plus a real-transcript smoke, and settings.json is backed up. So the build is done on Opus.

But your instinct is the right one, redirected: the Fable-worthy move is not a rebuild, it is an adversarial hardening pass on it. This is load-bearing infra that gates every response and fails open silently, so a subtle regression (a banned glyph or bad path the consolidation lets through that an original would have caught) degrades all your shareable output without anyone noticing. That is the CE signal multiplied by reach, exactly the profile that earns Fable-grade scrutiny even though the surface looks like a shell script. My author-review is biased; I want a strong independent reviewer hunting regressions.

Launching that now, and taking a deliberate exception to my own 2-concurrent cap (making it three): the 2-cap targets parallel whole-corpus long-turns that drop, and this is a bounded read-one-script review, a different and lower risk class. If it drops, the resume pattern recovers it. I am also capturing your blast-radius insight into the rubric, because it is a real refinement: reach amplifies cost-of-error.

input.description: Fable hardening review of linter
input.name: fable-lint-hardening
input.prompt:
You are Claude Fable 5. Adversarially harden a piece of load-bearing infrastructure: a consolidated Stop-hook response linter that gates EVERY response Brien's Claude Code sessions emit. A silent regression here degrades all of his shareable output going forward, so the cost of a missed bug is high and the catch-net is weak (the hook fails open by design, so a bug is silent). Be maximally skeptical.

WHAT IT IS. /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/response-lint-stop-check.sh consolidates three previously separate Stop hooks into one, running four rules over the last assistant message in a single transcript parse:
  - emdash (BLOCK): banned dash/arrow/ellipsis glyphs
  - link-format (BLOCK): non-clickable local path forms (fenced code blocks exempt)
  - presend-assertion (BLOCK): client send-ready content without an assertion-audit marker
  - model-effort (WARN-ONLY): substantive turn missing a "Model . Effort" recommendation line
The consolidation goal was BYTE-IDENTICAL enforcement for the three BLOCK rules versus their predecessors.

READ, to judge fidelity and find holes:
  - the consolidated script: /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/response-lint-stop-check.sh
  - the three predecessors it replaced: /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/emdash-stop-check.sh , /Users/brien/.claude/hooks/link-format-stop-check.sh , /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/presend-assertion-check.sh
  - the test harness: /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/frameworks/intent/hooks/tests/test_response_lint.py

HUNT FOR, ranked by how much you should care:
  1. REGRESSIONS: any input the consolidated version lets THROUGH that an original would have BLOCKED (a banned glyph or bad path that now slips into Brien's output). This is the worst failure mode. Construct concrete failing inputs and show why the original caught it and the consolidation does not.
  2. Fail-open holes: paths where an exception or malformed transcript silently skips enforcement.
  3. Rule-interaction bugs: one rule's text prep corrupting another's; combined-block formatting problems.
  4. Regex edge cases per rule versus its original (anchoring, code-fence stripping, the backtick and tilde patterns, the presend SEND vs AUDIT markers, case handling).
  5. model-effort heuristic quality: its false-positive and false-negative profile (the 800-char threshold, the head-of-message marker regex). It is warn-only so lower stakes, but characterize it.
  6. Transcript-format edge cases: tool-only assistant messages, multiple assistant messages in a turn, empty or string content, missing fields.
  7. Test-coverage gaps: what the harness does NOT exercise that it should.

DELIVER a findings report ranked by severity, each finding with a concrete failing input and a recommended fix, REGRESSIONS first. If you find no regressions, say so explicitly and state your confidence and what you checked to reach it. Do NOT modify the hook or the test; this is review-only, because Brien's session applies fixes so a human stays in the loop on live enforcement infrastructure.

PERSIST INCREMENTALLY to /Users/brien/Workspaces/Core/products/_intake/2026-07-18-response-lint-hardening-review/ . Create the directory. Write review.md as soon as it is complete, then a one-line STATUS.md. Get the file on disk promptly so a connection drop cannot wipe the turn.

BOUNDARIES. Read-only on ALL files including the hook and the test. Write only inside that review directory. Do not commit. Do not spawn subagents. Internal IP, no redaction. Never use em-dashes, en-dashes, arrow glyphs, or the ellipsis character (use periods, commas, colons, parentheses, or the words "to" and "vs"). Every file reference is a bare full-absolute /Users/brien/... path, no backticks, no markdown links.

If you hit any gate (permission, credential, usage-credits), stop and report it as your final message.

Final message is a data return: (a) the review path, (b) the count of REGRESSIONS found (the critical class), (c) the single most severe finding in one line, (d) any gate hit.

This multiplies the CE signal by reach and pushes such work toward Fable-grade scrutiny (at minimum an adversarial review pass), regardless of how simple the artifact looks. Reach is a CE multiplier.

**Route away from Fable:**
